***Downton Abbey: A New Era*** is a 2022 [historical drama film](/wiki/Historical_drama_film "Historical drama film") directed by [Simon Curtis](/wiki/Simon_Curtis_(filmmaker) "Simon Curtis (filmmaker)") from a screenplay by [Julian Fellowes](/wiki/Julian_Fellowes "Julian Fellowes"). It is the sequel to *[Downton Abbey](/wiki/Downton_Abbey_(film) "Downton Abbey (film)")* (2019) and the second film in the [*Downton Abbey* franchise](/wiki/Downton_Abbey "Downton Abbey"). The third and final film, *[Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale](/wiki/Downton_Abbey:_The_Grand_Finale "Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale")*, was released on 12 September 2025.

Set in 1928, this film follows the Crawley family on a journey to the south of France to uncover the mystery of the Dowager Countess's newly inherited villa.

After the release of the first film in 2019, Fellowes and the cast stated that they already had ideas about doing a sequel. In January 2020, it was reported that Fellowes would begin working on the film, and later that September, it was revealed that the script for the film had been written. Filming began in mid-April 2021, and ended in July, with a production budget of $40 million. The title was announced the following month.

*Downton Abbey: A New Era* premiered at [Odeon Luxe Leicester Square](/wiki/Odeon_Luxe_Leicester_Square "Odeon Luxe Leicester Square") in London on 25 April 2022 and was released in the United Kingdom on 29 April and in the United States on 20 May by [Focus Features](/wiki/Focus_Features "Focus Features").[[6]](#cite_note-deadline-jan22-change-6) The film received generally positive reviews from critics and grossed $92.7 million worldwide.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1928, Tom Branson, the [Earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl") of Grantham's widowed son-in-law, marries Lucy Smith, the illegitimate daughter of and sole heir to Lady Maud Bagshaw, [Queen Mary's](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") [lady-in-waiting](/wiki/Lady-in-waiting "Lady-in-waiting"). Violet Crawley, the Dowager Countess of Grantham reveals that years ago the recently deceased [Marquis](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess") de Montmirail had given her a [Southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France") villa. Violet is bequeathing it to her great-granddaughter, Sybbie, the daughter of Tom and the late Lady Sybil Crawley (Branson), to ensure that she is more socially and financially on par with her cousins George, Caroline, Marigold, and Peter.

A [film production company](/wiki/British_Lion_Films "British Lion Films") wants to use Downton to shoot a silent film, *The Gambler*. Robert Crawley (Lord Grantham) declines, but eldest daughter and estate manager Lady Mary Talbot says the fee would replace Downton's leaking roof. The household staff are eager to see the film's stars, but lead actress Myrna Dalgliesh's haughtiness is offensive.

The new Marquis de Montmirail invites the Crawley family to visit the villa. The ailing Violet is unable to travel, but Tom and Lucy, Lady Bagshaw, Robert Crawley and wife Cora, their daughter Edith and her husband, Bertie Pelham, the [Marquess](/wiki/Marquesses_in_the_United_Kingdom "Marquesses in the United Kingdom") of Hexham, accept. Former butler Carson, valet Mr Bates, and lady's maid Miss Baxter accompany the family. Lady Edith, once again writing for the London-based magazine she owns, uses the trip as a working assignment. Lady Mary remains at Downton to oversee the film crew, assisted by Mrs Carson, the former Mrs Hughes.

The new Marquis de Montmirail welcomes the family, though his mother contests Violet Crawley's ownership. However, the family lawyer states that there is no basis for a claim. The Marquis later stuns Robert by implying that Robert's birth date, nine months after the Dowager Countess's 1864 visit, could mean they are half-brothers. That night, Cora tells Robert she may have a fatal illness. Robert breaks down at the prospect of losing his mother, the Crawley name and title, and his wife in short succession.

At Downton, the studio cancels *The Gambler* because silent films have become unprofitable in the new "[talkie](/wiki/Talkie "Talkie")" era. Lady Mary suggests salvaging the project by [dubbing](/wiki/Dubbing "Dubbing") in the dialogue for completed scenes and filming new ones with sound. Lead actor Guy Dexter's resonant voice is suitable, but Myrna Dalgleish's marked [cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney") accent is inappropriate for her upper-class character. Mrs Hughes suggests that Lady Mary dub Dalgleish's voice. Fearing her career is over, Dalgleish wants to walk away, but Downton servants Anna and Daisy persuade her to complete the film. Former Downton footman Mr Molesley, who can [lip-read](/wiki/Lip_reading "Lip reading"), reconstructs the dialogue for dubbing and creates a dialogued script. During production, Dexter tacitly reveals to Downton's [closeted](/wiki/Closeted "Closeted") butler, Thomas Barrow, that he is also gay. Dexter offers Barrow a position as his housekeeper and wardrobe assistant in Hollywood.

The family returns to Downton while filming continues. Lady Mary gently rebuffs director Jack Barber's flirtations, although her husband Henry's prolonged absence for a car rally has strained their marriage. Dr Clarkson diagnoses Cora with [pernicious anaemia](/wiki/Pernicious_anaemia "Pernicious anaemia"), a treatable condition. Cora helps Dalgleish develop an American accent, potentially saving her career. Edith, unfulfilled and constrained as a marchioness, intends to resume running her London-based magazine. Wanting more privacy, newlywed servants Daisy and Andy successfully scheme to match Daisy's former father-in-law, Mr Mason, and Downton cook Mrs Patmore. When the unpaid movie extras walk out, the Downton staff replace them. Barber offers Molesley a lucrative deal as a [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter"). Molesley then proposes to Baxter, unaware they are heard on an open microphone.

Violet assures Robert that the late Lord Grantham was his father and that nothing occurred between her and Montmirail. Violet physically deteriorates and dies surrounded by family and servants. After Barrow resigns as butler to accept Dexter's offer, Mary asks Carson to train footman Andy as Barrow's replacement. Months later, Tom and Lucy, along with Sybbie, return to Downton with their newborn infant. A new portrait of Violet hangs in the entry hall.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

After the release of the first film in 2019, creator [Julian Fellowes](/wiki/Julian_Fellowes "Julian Fellowes") and the cast stated that they already had ideas about doing a sequel.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In January 2020, it was reported that Fellowes would begin working on it after he finished scripting drama series [*The Gilded Age*](/wiki/The_Gilded_Age_(TV_series) "The Gilded Age (TV series)").[[19]](#cite_note-19) In September 2020, [Jim Carter](/wiki/Jim_Carter_(actor) "Jim Carter (actor)"), who plays Carson, said that the script for the sequel had been written,[[20]](#cite_note-20) and in February 2021, [Hugh Bonneville](/wiki/Hugh_Bonneville "Hugh Bonneville"), who plays Robert, stated in an interview with [BBC Radio 2](/wiki/BBC_Radio_2 "BBC Radio 2") that once the cast and crew had been [vaccinated](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine") for [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"), the film would be made.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") was originally scheduled to take place from 12 June to 12 August 2021, in [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), England,[[22]](#cite_note-VarietyInsight-22) but *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* confirmed that production had started in mid-April 2021.[[13]](#cite_note-DH-13)
On 16 July 2021, [Elizabeth McGovern](/wiki/Elizabeth_McGovern "Elizabeth McGovern") announced on [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") that she had completed filming.[[23]](#cite_note-23) On 25 August 2021, the film's title *Downton Abbey: A New Era* was announced.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The principal cast of the first film returned. Joining the cast are [Hugh Dancy](/wiki/Hugh_Dancy "Hugh Dancy"), [Laura Haddock](/wiki/Laura_Haddock "Laura Haddock"), [Nathalie Baye](/wiki/Nathalie_Baye "Nathalie Baye"), [Dominic West](/wiki/Dominic_West "Dominic West") and [Jonathan Zaccaï](/wiki/Jonathan_Zacca%C3%AF "Jonathan Zaccaï").[[13]](#cite_note-DH-13)

## Soundtrack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Soundtrack")]

Main article: [Downton Abbey: A New Era (soundtrack)](/wiki/Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era_(soundtrack) "Downton Abbey: A New Era (soundtrack)")

John Lunn returned to score music for *A New Era* after previously doing the same for the television series and its film continuation.[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26) The score performed by the Chamber Orchestra of London with vocals performed by [Cherise Adams-Burnett](/wiki/Cherise_Adams-Burnett "Cherise Adams-Burnett"), was released on 29 April 2022 on CD, digital download and vinyl by [Decca Gold](/wiki/Decca_Gold "Decca Gold"), [Decca Records](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") and [Universal Music](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group").[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Release")]

*Downton Abbey: A New Era* was scheduled originally to be released in cinemas on 22 December 2021,[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) before having its release date moved to 18 March 2022,[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30) and subsequently to 29 April in the UK, and 20 May in North America.[[6]](#cite_note-deadline-jan22-change-6) The film premiered at [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square "Leicester Square") in London on 25 April 2022.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The film was originally set to stream in the United States on [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)") on 4 July, 45 days after its American theatrical release,[[33]](#cite_note-33) but it was released to the streaming service on 24 June 2022, earlier than planned.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

It was released on [Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray"), [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") and [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and digital download on 5 July 2022 by [Universal Pictures Home Entertainment](/wiki/Universal_Pictures_Home_Entertainment "Universal Pictures Home Entertainment") (through Studio Distribution Services LLC) in the United States, and was released on Blu-ray and DVD on 15 August 2022 by Universal through [Warner Bros. Home Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Home_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Home Entertainment") in the United Kingdom.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Downton Abbey: A New Era* grossed $44.2 million in the United States and Canada and $48.5 million in other countries, for a worldwide total of $92.7 million.[[4]](#cite_note-BOM-4)[[5]](#cite_note-NUM-5)

In the United States and Canada, the film was released alongside *[Men](/wiki/Men_(2022_film) "Men (2022 film)")*, and was projected to gross $16–21 million from 3,820 cinemas over its opening weekend.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) It made $7.4 million on its first day, including $1 million from Thursday night previews. The film went on to debut at $16 million and finished second at the box office,[[37]](#cite_note-37) thus opening at the lower end of projections. 48% of the opening weekend audience was over the age of 55 and *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* said it "repped the first time [exhibitors] saw older patrons since pre-pandemic".[[3]](#cite_note-opening-3) In its second weekend the film fell 64% to $5.8 million (and a total of $7.4 million over the four-day [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day") frame), finishing fourth.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The large drop was attributed in-part to newcomer *[Top Gun: Maverick](/wiki/Top_Gun:_Maverick "Top Gun: Maverick")*, whose audience was 55% over the age of 35, sharing much of the same demographics as *A New Era*.[[40]](#cite_note-40) It then made $3.2 million in its third weekend,[[41]](#cite_note-41) $1.8 million in its fourth,[[42]](#cite_note-42) and $828,265 in its fifth,[[43]](#cite_note-43) before dropping out of the box office top ten in its sixth.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Outside of the United States and Canada, the film earned $9.3 million from 33 markets in its opening weekend. This included $3.8 million in the United Kingdom from 746 cinemas (the second-widest release of all time, after *[No Time to Die](/wiki/No_Time_to_Die "No Time to Die")* in 2021), and $1.3 million in Australia.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The film added $6.6 million in its second weekend,[[46]](#cite_note-46) $3.6 million in its third,[[47]](#cite_note-47) $3.8 million in its fourth,[[48]](#cite_note-48) and $2.2 million in its fifth.[[49]](#cite_note-49) It made an additional $715,000 in its seventh weekend.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 86% of 198 critics' reviews are positive, with an average rating of 6.9/10. The website's consensus reads: "While *Downton Abbey*'s frothiness comes close to curdling into outright suds, *A New Era*'s familiar comforts will please longtime fans."[[51]](#cite_note-Rotten_Tomatoes-51) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigned the film a score of 63 out of 100, based on 46 critics, indicating "generally favorable" reviews.[[52]](#cite_note-Metacritic-52) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A" on an A+ to F scale (same as the first film), while [PostTrak](/wiki/PostTrak "PostTrak") reported 93% of audience members gave it a positive score, with 79% saying they would definitely recommend it.[[3]](#cite_note-opening-3)

### Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Accolades")]

*Downton Abbey: A New Era* was nominated for [Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Golden_Rooster_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Golden Rooster Award for Best Foreign Language Film") at the [35th Golden Rooster Awards](/wiki/35th_Golden_Rooster_Awards "35th Golden Rooster Awards") in 2022 but lost to the Serbian film *[Father](/wiki/Father_(2020_film) "Father (2020 film)")*.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Downton_Abbey:_A_New_Era&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sequel")]

Main article: [Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale](/wiki/Downton_Abbey:_The_Grand_Finale "Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale")

On 13 May 2024 it was announced that a third film was in production, with much of the cast returning. [Joely Richardson](/wiki/Joely_Richardson "Joely Richardson"), [Alessandro Nivola](/wiki/Alessandro_Nivola "Alessandro Nivola"), [Simon Russell Beale](/wiki/Simon_Russell_Beale "Simon Russell Beale") and [Arty Froushan](/wiki/Arty_Froushan "Arty Froushan") will appear as new characters.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
